Life as We Know It (2010)

Theatrical Performance
Domestic Box Office $53,374,681Details
International Box Office $52,513,156
Worldwide Box Office $105,887,837
Domestic Physical Disc Sales
DVD Sales (estimated) $20,485,685
Blu-ray Sales (estimated) $3,417,452
Total $23,903,137
Further financial details...

Synopsis

After a disastrous first date, the only thing they have in common is their dislike for each other and their love for their goddaughter, Sophie. But when they suddenly become all Sophie has in the world, they are forced to put their differences aside. Juggling career ambitions and competing social calendars, they’ll have to find some common ground while living under one roof — and deal with life as we know it.

Metrics

Opening Weekend:$14,506,464 (27.2% of total gross)
Legs:3.68 (domestic box office/biggest weekend)
Domestic Share:50.4% (domestic box office/worldwide)
Production Budget:$38,000,000 (worldwide box office is 2.8 times production budget)
Theater counts:3,150 opening theaters/3,150 max. theaters, 5.5 weeks average run per theater
Infl. Adj. Dom. BO $80,903,887

Movie Details

Domestic Releases: October 8th, 2010 (Wide) by Warner Bros.
Video Release: February 8th, 2011 by Warner Home Video, released as Life As We Know It
MPA Rating: PG-13 for sexual material, language and some drug content
(Rating bulletin 2125, 6/16/2010)
Running Time: 113 minutes
Plot point: Dysfunctional Family, Orphan, Romance, Unexpected Families
Subgenre: Romantic Comedy
Source:Original Screenplay
Genre:Romantic Comedy
Production Method:Live Action
Creative Type:Contemporary Fiction
Production/Financing Companies: Josephson Entertainment, Gold Circle Films, Village Roadshow Productions
Production Countries: United States
Languages: English

Synopsis

After a disastrous first date, the only thing they have in common is their dislike for each other and their love for their goddaughter, Sophie. But when they suddenly become all Sophie has in the world, they are forced to put their differences aside. Juggling career ambitions and competing social calendars, they’ll have to find some common ground while living under one roof — and deal with life as we know it.
